dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for unconscious patient at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Rachel, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
We found him on the ground, unconscious and not breathing well.
Patient is unresponsive-no eye, verbal, or motor response.
No eye movement observed during assessment.
No verbal or vocal response when approached.
No motor response when stimulus was applied, per GCS score of 3.
Airway is patent as per patient report at scene, but respiratory effort is inadequate.
Respirations are shallow according to initial findings in patient report.
Weak radial pulse is noted with heart rate initially recorded at 92 per minute, per on-scene documentation.
Skin is pale, cool, and moist per paramedic assessment as reported in the ePCR narrative.
Blood pressure cannot be obtained at this time due to patient's critical acuity, as documented in the ePCR.
Oxygen saturation was recorded at 94% upon first assessment and not supplemented at that time, as written in ePCR.
Inserting 28-fr nasopharyngeal airway into the right nostril based on current procedural documentation.
Assisted ventilation started with bag-valve mask as per [CITY] Fire Department's prior assistance mentioned in the narrative.
Restraints applied as preparation for possible post-naloxone response as noted in field care documentation.
At 17:50, vitals are HR of 96, RR of 16, BP of 100/80, SpO2 of 98, and sinus rhythm on ECG.
Glucose levels could not be obtained due to patient being unconscious and unable to cooperate as stated in ePCR.
Administering 2 mg intranasal naloxone per protocol. First dose had no response as documented in ePCR.
4-lead ECG monitoring initiated at 17:48 and showed sinus tachycardia, as per ePCR timestamps.
O2 saturation improved to 98% with assisted ventilation via face mask, as recorded in ePCR.
Capnography applied via nasal placement to monitor waveform etCO2 levels, consistent with ePCR narrative.
12-lead ECG at 17:52 interpreted as normal sinus rhythm for a 40+ male, as stated in ePCR.
No pain assessment performed due to patient's unresponsiveness and field impression of opioid overdose, as per ePCR.
Unable to obtain personal information due to unconsciousness and no ID available, reported in ePCR narrative.
No known past medical history or medications listed in the report from the scene.
Following protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose and administered two 2 mg doses of naloxone with no response, per incident logs.
At 17:55, vitals noted as HR 96, RR 16, SpO2 98%, and sinus tach.
Ongoing assisted ventilation and monitoring continued; no interventions beyond that initiated prior to transport.
Beginning emergent transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for advanced treatment due to critical acuity.
Patient is secured in supine position using a four-person lift and H-style harness as documented in ePCR.
Stretcher secured using the manufacturer's latching system as stated in the care documentation narrative.
At 17:57, vitals are HR 96, RR 16, SpO2 97%, and sinus rhythm still showing.
Administering second naloxone dose due to no patient response, per ePCR records.
No further interventions initiated before transport, as per ongoing care recorded in ePCR.
Stretcher in ambulance, with restraints and all safety belts in place for transport as recorded in ePCR.
Pale, cool, and moist skin remains, no abrasions or rashes per field observation and BLS assessment.
Pupils are non-reactive and 1 mm in size, as described in field narrative.
No visible nasal discharge or oral abnormalities as reported in med-tech narrative.
No open chest injuries observed; EKG showing normal rhythm, as mentioned in care documents.
No signs of distension or guarding noted per field assessment, as patient was not moved.
No observable limb or spine abnormalities per ePCR narrative.
Stretcher secured for movement with 4-lead monitoring in place.
Last readings prior to transport confirmed stable vitals for a critical patient.
Manufacturer latching system checked for full compliance as per protocol.
Patient remains critical at ED arrival, as reported per handoff documentation.
Care was supervised by 8027 with full reporting and logs as stated in ePCR.
Complete report given to Yager, RN at the ED with all interventions and findings detailed in patient report.
Relevant patient history relayed via HEAR communication, as per ePCR handoff notes.
Draw-sheet transfer used with rails raised for patient safety and room access.
Full documentation completed and filed in EMR for emergency care compliance.
No patient response after two naloxone doses, as noted by paramedics at handoff.
All protocol items passed to ED team for follow-up care after transport.
Final readings at transport confirm vitals remain stable.
ED alerted of patient's condition and informed of naloxone administration and EKG results.
All transport safety measures completed prior to arrival at [CITY].
Patient still secured during transport and monitored per latest reports.
Confirming skin findings as stated in all previous documentation accounts.
Unresponsive with pinpoint pupils as listed in field evaluation.
No immediate signs of obstruction or discharge, as per BLS and paramedic notes.
EKG findings remain consistent, no irregularities found on rechecks.
Continues without palpable masses or rigidity noted in transport logs.
No extremity issues noted in narrative.
Final assessment for vitals and interventions before ED arrival.
Rechecking vitals at final approach to hospital.
Pulse remains 96, as noted in the final ePCR before handoff.
Respiration rate is steady at 16 per minute.
BP maintained at 100/80 as last documented.
SpO2 at 97%, stable in transport.
EKG still read as sinus rhythm in final update.
No further interventions needed before arrival.
Initiating ED bed transfer using drawsheet method as recorded.
Final loading of stretcher into [COMPANY_NAME].
Final assessment performed and passed off to ED staff.
Patient status reviewed and received by ER nursing staff.
Bag-valve-mask, naloxone documentation, and EKG logs handed off with patient.
Full care transition reviewed and signed by receiving nurse as per logs.
Patient placed into [COMPANY_NAME], ER room 9, for critical care.
Patient remains unresponsive as noted at handoff.
All protocol actions shared and confirmed with hospital staff.
Stretcher is locked post-movement for ED safety checks.
Patient moved with drawsheet to bed with both side rails up as per protocol.
Vital signs remain stable with no acute worsening noted.
Emergent movement completed per emergency care standard.
Patient status updated for ER staff as final step.
All emergency care actions shared with receiving medical team.
Bed secured for patient safe transfer to hospital bed.
All care and documentation finalized for hospital care team.
Handoff report completed and signed by transfer nurse receiving care.
Rescue equipment left with patient on stretcher prior to entering hospital.
Full treatment care will now continue within [COMPANY_NAME].
Protocol 7-1 transfer of care to ED completed.
Patient status and all interventions checked as complete.
Final remark on patient stability and critical condition reported to ED nurse.
All safety measures confirmed for EMT to exit and close transport report.
All last assessments confirmed for safe ED transition.
Final patient handoff complete and accepted by [COMPANY_NAME].
EMT exits ambulance and closes report.
Entire care and transfer protocol finalized for the patient.
